preamble.summary: A psalm of urgent deliverance, attributed to David when Saul sent men to watch his house in order to kill him. David pleads for rescue from enemies who prowl like dogs around the city at evening, snarling and howling. Two refrains structure the psalm: 'You, LORD, laugh at them' and 'They return at evening, snarling like dogs, prowling the city.' The psalm moves from desperate plea to triumphant confidence in God as fortress and faithful love.
preamble.remarkable: The dog-imagery is the psalm's signature feature (vv. 7, 15). The enemies are not described as warriors or lions but as feral dogs — pack animals that scavenge at the margins of the city after dark. In the ancient Near East, dogs were not pets but despised scavengers associated with filth, disease, and shamelessness. To compare the king's soldiers to prowling dogs is to strip them of all dignity. They serve a paranoid king and do his dirty work at night, like animals foraging in garbage. The double use of this image (vv. 7 and 15) creates a structural refrain that bookends the psalm's center, but with a crucial shift: in verse 7 the dogs are threatening; by verse 15 they are merely pathetic, whimpering and unfed.
preamble.friction: The superscription refers to 1 Samuel 19:11, where Saul sent messengers to David's house to watch it overnight and kill him in the morning. Michal, David's wife, helped him escape through a window. The psalm's reference to 'all the nations' (v. 6, 9) seems broader than a domestic assassination attempt, leading some scholars to see the psalm as adapting a personal crisis into a national liturgy. The shift between singular and plural enemies, and between personal and international scope, may reflect this liturgical expansion.
preamble.connections: The historical setting is 1 Samuel 19:11-17, where Michal lowers David through a window and deceives Saul's men with a household idol in the bed. The dog-pack imagery connects to 1 Kings 14:11, 16:4, and 21:23-24, where dogs eating the dead is the mark of the most dishonorable death. The fortress-language (misgav, 'refuge, high place') connects to Psalm 18:2 and 2 Samuel 22:3. The 'God of my faithful love' (Elohei chasdi) in verse 18 is a remarkable designation found nowhere else in the Psalter.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֣חַ אַל־תַּ֭שְׁחֵת לְדָוִ֣ד מִכְתָּ֑ם בִּשְׁלֹ֤חַ שָׁא֗וּל וַֽיִּשְׁמְר֥וּ אֶת־הַ֝בַּ֗יִת לַהֲמִיתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, Altaschith, Michtam of David; when Saul sent, and they watched the house to kill him.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. "Do Not Destroy."
A miktam of David, when Saul sent men
to watch the house in order to kill him.
translator_notes: The third consecutive al tashchet ('do not destroy') psalm. The superscription specifies the crisis: Saul dispatched agents to surveil David's house overnight, planning to execute him at dawn (1 Samuel 19:11). The verb shamar ('to watch, to guard') — normally a positive word (God as guardian) — is here inverted: the watchers are predators, not protectors.

verses.text_kjv: Deliver me from mine enemies, O my God: defend me from them that rise up against me.
verses.rendering:
Deliver me from my enemies, my God;
set me safely on high, away from those who rise against me.
translator_notes: tesaggeveni ('set me on high, make me inaccessibly high') from sagav — to be high, to be exalted beyond reach. David asks not just for rescue but for elevation — to be placed somewhere his enemies cannot reach. The misgav ('high refuge') language that will define this psalm begins here.

verses.text_hebrew: הַצִּילֵ֥נִי מִפֹּ֣עֲלֵי אָ֑וֶן וּמֵאַנְשֵׁ֖י דָמִ֣ים הוֹשִׁיעֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Deliver me from the workers of iniquity, and save me from bloody men.
verses.rendering:
Deliver me from workers of wickedness,
and save me from men of blood.
translator_notes: po'alei aven ('workers of wickedness') — the same phrase from Psalm 53:5. anshei damim ('men of blood') — those whose hands are stained with bloodshed, the same phrase from Psalm 55:24. The vocabulary recycling across this psalm cluster creates a coherent picture of the enemies David faces.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י הִנֵּ֨ה ׀ אָרְב֣וּ לְנַפְשִׁי֮ יָג֥וּרוּ עָלַ֗י עַ֫זִּ֥ים לֹא־פִשְׁעִ֥י וְלֹא־חַטָּאתִ֗י יְהֹוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For, lo, they lie in wait for my soul: the mighty are gathered against me; not for my transgression, nor for my sin, O LORD.
translator_notes: David declares his innocence using two of the three sin-words from Psalm 51: pesha ('rebellion') and chattat ('sin'). He is not claiming perfection — he is asserting that his enemies' hostility has no just cause. Their attack is not punishment for wrongdoing but unprovoked aggression against an innocent man. The protest lo pish'i ve-lo chattati ('not my rebellion and not my sin') is a legal defense before the divine judge.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּלִי־עָ֭וֺן יְרוּצ֣וּן וְיִכּוֹנָ֑נוּ ע֖וּרָה לִקְרָאתִ֣י וּרְאֵֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: They run and prepare themselves without my fault: awake to help me, and behold.
verses.rendering:
Without guilt on my part they rush and take position.
Wake up to meet me — and see!
translator_notes: The enemies yerutsun ('run, rush') and yikkonanu ('prepare themselves, take position') — military language for taking battle stations. And they do this beli avon ('without guilt') — David has given them no cause. The imperative urah ('wake up!') is bold — David speaks to God as though God is sleeping through the crisis. This is not irreverence but desperation.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאַתָּ֤ה יְהֹוָֽה־אֱלֹהִ֨ים ׀ צְבָא֗וֹת אֱלֹהֵ֣י יִ֭שְׂרָאֵל הָקִ֨יצָה לִפְקֹ֣ד כׇּל־הַגּוֹיִ֑ם אַל־תָּחֹ֨ן כׇּל־בֹּ֖גְדֵי אָ֣וֶן סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou therefore, O LORD God of hosts, the God of Israel, awake to visit all the heathen: be not merciful to any wicked transgressors. Selah.
verses.rendering:
And you — LORD God of Armies,
God of Israel —
rouse yourself to punish all the nations!
Show no mercy to any treacherous evildoer.
Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: David invokes God's fullest titles: YHWH Elohim Tseva'ot ('LORD God of Armies') — the commander of heaven's military forces — and Elohei Yisra'el ('God of Israel') — the covenant God bound to his people. The request is that God haqitsah ('rouse himself, wake up') to paqad ('visit, punish, attend to') kol ha-goyim ('all the nations'). The scope has expanded from Saul's household thugs to international judgment. The command al tachon ('show no mercy to') applies to kol bogdei aven ('all treacherous evildoers') — bogdei from bagad ('to betray, to act treacherously'), combined with aven ('wickedness'). These are not merely hostile people but faithless betrayers.
translator_notes: The expansion to 'all the nations' may reflect liturgical adaptation — what began as David's personal crisis became a prayer for God's cosmic justice. Alternatively, David may be placing his personal enemies within the larger framework of all who oppose God's purposes.

verses.text_hebrew: יָשׁ֣וּבוּ לָ֭עֶרֶב יֶהֱמ֥וּ כַכָּ֗לֶב וִיס֥וֹבְב֗וּ עִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: They return at evening: they make a noise like a dog, and go round about the city.
verses.rendering:
They return at evening,
snarling like dogs,
prowling around the city.
verses.expanded_rendering: The first occurrence of the dog-refrain. The enemies yashuvu la-erev ('return at evening') — they operate at night, like predators that hunt after dark. They yehemu ka-kalev ('howl/snarl like a dog') — the verb hamah means 'to growl, to moan, to make noise,' and the comparison to dogs is deliberately degrading. In the ancient Near East, dogs were unclean scavengers, associated with garbage heaps and carrion. These soldiers of the king are reduced to feral animals. And they yesovevu ir ('circle the city') — prowling the perimeter, looking for a way in. The image captures both menace and pathetic futility: they circle but cannot find their prey.
translator_notes: The evening timing connects to the superscription — Saul's men watched David's house overnight (1 Samuel 19:11). The dogs that prowl at dusk are these same watchers, waiting for dawn to make their kill. Michal's intervention (lowering David through the window) happened during the night these dogs were circling.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֤ה ׀ יַבִּ֘יע֤וּן בְּפִיהֶ֗ם חֲ֭רָבוֹת בְּשִׂפְתוֹתֵיהֶ֑ם כִּי־מִ֥י שֹׁמֵֽעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, they belch out with their mouth: swords are in their lips: for who, say they, doth hear?
verses.rendering:
Look what pours from their mouths —
swords on their lips!
For they say, "Who is listening?"
translator_notes: yabbiun ('they pour out, they gush, they belch') — their mouths overflow with violent speech. charavot be-siftotehem ('swords on their lips') — the sword-tongue image from Psalm 57:5 continues. Their confidence is grounded in the assumption mi shomea ('who is listening?') — practical atheism again (cf. Psalm 53:2). They believe no one — not God, not a higher authority — hears or cares what they say.

verses.text_kjv: But thou, O LORD, shalt laugh at them; thou shalt have all the heathen in derision.
verses.rendering:
But you, LORD — you laugh at them;
you scoff at all the nations.
verses.expanded_rendering: The enemies asked 'who is listening?' — and the answer is devastating: God is not merely listening; he is laughing. tischaq lamo ('you laugh at them') — the same divine laughter from Psalm 2:4, where God in heaven laughs at the nations that conspire against his anointed. til'ag ('you scoff, you mock, you deride') — God treats their schemes with the contempt they deserve. The dogs prowl and snarl, but the God of the universe finds them amusing. This is not cruel mockery but the disproportion between finite threat and infinite power: the conspiracy of armed men against God's chosen one is, from heaven's perspective, a joke.
translator_notes: The divine laughter motif (Psalms 2:4, 37:13, 59:9) is one of the most striking anthropomorphisms in the Hebrew Bible. It expresses not emotional amusement but the absolute sovereignty of God over all human scheming. The enemies' self-importance is comic when viewed from God's throne.

verses.text_kjv: Because of his strength will I wait upon thee: for God is my defence.
verses.rendering:
My strength — I watch for you,
for God is my fortress.
translator_notes: The Qere reading is uzzi ('my strength') rather than uzzo ('his strength'). David addresses God as his strength and watches (eshmorah) for him — the same verb (shamar, 'to watch') that described the enemies watching his house in the superscription. They watch to kill; David watches for God. misgabbi ('my fortress, my high refuge') — the key word of the psalm, repeated in verses 10, 17, and 18.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱלֹהֵ֣י חַ֭סְדִּי יְקַדְּמֵ֑נִי אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים יַרְאֵ֥נִי בְשֹׁרְרָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The God of my mercy shall prevent me: God shall let me see my desire upon mine enemies.
verses.rendering:
My God of faithful love will come to meet me;
God will let me look in triumph on my enemies.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase Elohei chasdi ('my God of faithful love' or 'the God of my chesed') is extraordinary — it appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. David does not say 'God of chesed' (a God characterized by faithful love) but 'God of my chesed' — a God whose faithful love is personally directed, specifically allocated, individually addressed. God's chesed is not generic goodness but targeted commitment to David. This God yeqaddemeni ('comes to meet me, goes before me') — the verb qadam means to arrive first, to precede. God's faithful love gets there before David does, before the crisis reaches him. And God will let David look (yar'eni) on his enemies — not with vengeance but with the calm gaze of someone who knows the outcome.
translator_notes: Elohei chasdi is a hapax-level construction — the possessive 'my faithful love' applied to God creates a unique intimacy. God is not just characterized by chesed in general but is bound to David by a specific, personal, covenantal faithful love.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenant faithfulness, kindness, mercy, devotion
key_terms.note: The construction Elohei chasdi ('my God of faithful love') personalizes chesed to the highest degree. God's covenant loyalty is not abstract but addressed to David specifically. This is the relational foundation of the entire psalm.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תַּהַרְגֵ֤ם ׀ פֶּ֥ן יִשְׁכְּח֗וּ עַ֫מִּ֥י הֲנִיעֵ֣מוֹ בְ֭חֵילְךָ וְהוֹרִידֵ֑מוֹ מָֽגִנֵּ֗נוּ אֲדֹנָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Slay them not, lest my people forget: scatter them by thy power; and bring them down, O Lord our shield.
verses.rendering:
Do not kill them, or my people will forget.
Scatter them by your power and bring them low,
Lord, our shield.
verses.expanded_rendering: A surprising request: al tahargemm ('do not kill them'). David asks God not to destroy his enemies outright, and the reason is pedagogical: pen yishkechu ammi ('lest my people forget'). If God annihilates the wicked instantly, the lesson will be forgotten. Instead: hani'emo ('make them wander, scatter them') and horidemo ('bring them down, bring them low'). David wants the enemies to become living examples of divine justice — wandering, diminished, visible proof of what happens to those who oppose God. Their slow decline will be more instructive than their sudden death. The address Adonai maginnenu ('Lord, our shield') — God is the defensive barrier that absorbs the attack.
translator_notes: This verse contains sophisticated theological reasoning about the purpose of divine judgment. Total destruction erases evidence; prolonged diminishment provides ongoing testimony. David wants his people to see the consequences play out over time so that the lesson becomes unforgettable.

verses.text_hebrew: חַטַּאת־פִּ֗ימוֹ דְּֽבַר־שְׂפָ֫תֵ֥ימוֹ וְיִלָּכְד֥וּ בִגְאוֹנָ֑ם וּמֵאָלָ֖ה וּמִכַּ֣חַשׁ יְסַפֵּֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For the sin of their mouth and the words of their lips let them even be taken in their pride: and for cursing and lying which they speak,
verses.rendering:
The sin of their mouths, the words of their lips —
let them be caught in their pride!
For the cursing and lies they speak,
translator_notes: The mouth-sin catalogue continues: chattat pimo ('the sin of their mouths'), devar sefateimo ('the word of their lips'), ge'onam ('their pride'), alah ('cursing, oath-breaking'), and kachash ('lies, denial'). The trap-vocabulary from Psalm 57:7 recurs: yillakhedu ('let them be caught') — their own words will become the net that ensnares them.

verses.text_hebrew: כַּלֵּ֥ה בְחֵמָ֗ה כַּ֫לֵּ֥ה וְאֵינֵ֑מוֹ וְֽיֵדְע֗וּ כִּי־אֱלֹהִ֥ים מֹשֵׁ֥ל בְּ֝יַעֲקֹ֗ב לְאַפְסֵי־הָאָ֥רֶץ סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Consume them in wrath, consume them, that they may not be: and let them know that God ruleth in Jacob unto the ends of the earth. Selah.
verses.rendering:
Consume them in wrath —
consume them until they are no more!
Let them know that God rules in Jacob,
to the ends of the earth.
Selah.
translator_notes: The doubled kalleh ('consume, finish off') intensifies the plea. But the purpose is not destruction for its own sake — it is revelation: ve-yed'u ('and let them know'). The goal of judgment is knowledge: ki Elohim moshel be-Ya'aqov ('that God rules in Jacob'). The scope: le-afsei ha-arets ('to the ends of the earth'). God's rule over Jacob (Israel) has universal implications — what he does for his covenant people declares his sovereignty everywhere.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיָשׁ֣וּבוּ לָ֭עֶרֶב יֶהֱמ֥וּ כַכָּ֗לֶב וִיס֥וֹבְב֗וּ עִֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: And at evening let them return; and let them make a noise like a dog, and go round about the city.
verses.rendering:
They return at evening,
snarling like dogs,
prowling around the city.
verses.expanded_rendering: The dog-refrain returns, but in the new context of verses 9-14 (God laughing, David trusting, judgment declared), the same image carries a different weight. The dogs are still prowling, still snarling — but now they are objects of divine laughter and inevitable judgment. The menace of verse 7 has become the futility of verse 15. The same dogs, the same city, the same evening — but David's perception has changed. The threat has not been removed; it has been reframed by trust.
translator_notes: The structural repetition of the refrain (vv. 7 and 15) is a deliberate literary technique. The listener hears the same words but understands them differently because of everything that has happened between the two occurrences. This is how trust transforms perception without changing circumstances.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵ֤מָּה ׀ יְנִיע֣וּן לֶ֭אֱכֹל אִם־לֹ֥א יִ֝שְׂבְּע֗וּ וַיָּלִֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let them wander up and down for meat, and grudge if they be not satisfied.
verses.rendering:
They wander about for food;
if they are not satisfied, they howl through the night.
translator_notes: The dogs are now scavengers — yeniu'un le-ekhol ('they wander for food'), searching for scraps. The mighty soldiers have become hungry strays. im lo yisbe'u vayyalinu ('if they are not satisfied, they spend the night howling') — unsatisfied dogs whimpering in the dark. The transformation from threatening to pathetic is complete.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֤י ׀ אָשִׁ֣יר עֻזֶּ֗ךָ וַאֲרַנֵּ֥ן לַבֹּ֗קֶר חַסְדֶּ֥ךָ כִּֽי־הָיִ֣יתָ מִשְׂגָּ֣ב לִ֑י וּ֝מָנ֗וֹס בְּי֣וֹם צַר־לִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But I will sing of thy power; yea, I will sing aloud of thy mercy in the morning: for thou hast been my defence and refuge in the day of my trouble.
verses.rendering:
But I will sing of your strength;
I will shout for joy at morning of your faithful love.
For you have been my fortress,
a refuge in the day of my distress.
verses.expanded_rendering: While the dogs howl through the night, David sings. The contrast is temporal: they prowl la-erev ('at evening'); David sings la-boqer ('at morning'). Night belongs to the dogs; morning belongs to the singer. What David sings about: uzzekha ('your strength') and chasdekha ('your faithful love'). Strength and chesed together — God is both powerful enough and loving enough to protect. The reason for confidence: hayita misgav li ('you have been my fortress') — the past tense confirms experience, not just hope. God has been the misgav ('high refuge, fortress') and manos ('place of escape, refuge') be-yom tsar li ('in the day of my distress'). David knows this because he has lived it.
translator_notes: The morning-singing in contrast to evening-prowling creates the psalm's final structural pair: darkness/light, dogs/singer, snarling/praise. David has survived the night. The dawn that Psalm 57:9 promised to wake is here.

verses.text_hebrew: עֻ֭זִּי אֵלֶ֣יךָ אֲזַמֵּ֑רָה כִּֽי־אֱלֹהִ֥ים מִ֝שְׂגַּבִּ֗י אֱלֹהֵ֥י חַסְדִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Unto thee, O my strength, will I sing: for God is my defence, and the God of my mercy.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse echoes verse 10 but transforms it. The watchful anxiety of verse 10 ('I watch for you') becomes the joyful singing of verse 18 ('to you I will sing'). And the psalm's last phrase repeats the extraordinary title from verse 11: Elohei chasdi ('my God of faithful love'). This is David's final word — not the enemies, not the dogs, not the night, but the God whose faithful love belongs personally to David. The psalm that began with assassination plots ends with a love song to the God who is both fortress and faithful lover.
translator_notes: The inclusio between verses 10 and 18 (both using uzzi, 'my strength,' and misgabbi, 'my fortress') closes the psalm in a ring structure. Everything between the two statements — the dogs, the laughter, the scattering, the consuming — is enclosed within the frame of 'God is my fortress.' The frame holds.
